verses.text: The chief fathers of the sons of Joseph came to Moses: the LORD commanded the inheritance of Zelophehad to be given to his daughters. But if they marry into another tribe, their inheritance shall be taken from us and added to theirs; at the jubile, the loss will be permanent. Guidance: A second land-law question from the same family that asked the first. The men of Manasseh are not contesting the daughters' right — they want to protect the tribe's edge of land. Good questions tend to spawn more good questions. God hears them all.

Guidance: A second land-law question from the same family that asked the first. The men of Manasseh are not contesting the daughters' right — they want to protect the tribe's edge of land. The chief fathers of the sons of Joseph came to Moses: the LORD commanded the inheritance of Zelophehad to be given to his daughters. But if they marry into another tribe, their inheritance shall be taken from us and added to theirs; at the jubile, the loss will be permanent. Guidance: A second land-law question from the same family that asked the first. The men of Manasseh are not contesting the daughters' right — they want to protect the tribe's edge of land. Good questions tend to spawn more good questions. God hears them all.

Moses by the word of the LORD: the tribe of Joseph hath said well. Let the daughters of Zelophehad marry to whom they think best — only to the family of the tribe of their father. So the inheritance shall not remove from tribe to tribe. Every daughter possessing an inheritance shall be wife unto one of her father's tribe. Guidance: A balanced ruling. The daughters keep their inheritance AND keep their choice — they may marry whom they think best, within the tribe. God's law preserves their agency and the tribe's land in one breath. The yes is bigger than the limit.

The yes is bigger than the limit.

As the LORD commanded Moses, so did the daughters of Zelophehad: Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, Noah were married unto their father's brothers' sons. They married into the families of Manasseh, and their inheritance remained in the tribe of Manasseh. Guidance: The five sisters obey, named one more time — God will not stop putting them in writing. They marry cousins, the tribe holds, and their father's name lives on. The book of Numbers ends with a wedding, not a war. Faithfulness writes the final word.

These are the commandments and the judgments which the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses unto the children of Israel in the plains of Moab by Jordan near Jericho. Guidance: Numbers closes with one sentence. Forty years of wandering, two censuses, a death sentence on a generation, and five sisters' inheritance — all distilled to: God spoke through Moses, by the Jordan, near Jericho. The river is right there. The book ends with the camp still on the east bank, waiting for the next leader to say cross.
